items.title: Split before any learned preprocessing
items.avoid: Fitting scalers, encoders, or feature selectors on the full dataset, then splitting.
items.prefer: Create train/val/test indices first; fit preprocessing pipelines on X_train only inside each experiment.
items.why: Preprocessing fit on hold-out rows leaks information — validation and test cease to be unseen.
items.example: Pipeline: indices = split(D) → ColumnTransformer.fit(X_train) → transform(X_val, X_test) → model.fit(X_train).

items.title: Lock the test set for final sign-off
items.avoid: Running hyperparameter grids ranked by test accuracy throughout the project.
items.prefer: Rank all trials on validation (or CV); document one final test evaluation when the model is frozen.
items.why: Repeated test-set use turns it into a validation set — the reported score is no longer an unbiased generalization estimate.
items.example: Experiment log: 47 trials scored on val F1; trial 31 wins; test F1 reported once in the release checklist.

items.title: Match split design to deployment
items.avoid: Default random shuffles for time series, recommender logs, or grouped medical records.
items.prefer: Temporal splits for forecasting, group splits for repeated entities, geographic splits for regional rollouts.
items.why: Generalization estimates are meaningful only when hold-out data resembles how unseen production examples arrive.
items.example: Train on users from markets A–C, validate on D, test on E before launching in market F.

items.title: Stratify imbalanced classification splits
items.avoid: Plain random splits on rare-event problems without checking class counts per partition.
items.prefer: Use stratify=y (or equivalent) so train, validation, and test each retain approximate class proportions.
items.why: A validation fold with zero positives makes F1 and threshold tuning meaningless — model selection becomes random.
items.example: 2% positive rate: stratified 70/15/15 keeps ~2% positives in each of the three sets.
